summary: I started my longshore career in 1967 after returning from Alaska. I was a member of the ILWU Local 32 and 52, and over the years, I worked on log ships and other cargo. The union was very important to me, especially during events like the 1971 ILWU strike and the Everett massacre. I eventually became involved in union leadership roles, including business manager and welfare director.

I am involved with the Pacific Coast Pensioners Association, which holds conventions like the one in Long Beach, California in 2017 where I was interviewed. The PCPA is important for retired longshore workers like myself, providing a community and support network. Being part of the PCPA allows me to stay connected with the union and fellow pensioners after my active working years.

I was raised in Everett, Washington by my aunt and uncle, who were Greek immigrants. Everett was a mill town with a strong union presence, which shaped my early life and community. I attended Everett High School and spent most of my life there, growing up in a union town with many mills and union families around me.

I was born in Seattle, Washington in 1942 but grew up in Everett. I remember some events in Seattle like building a bridge over the Seattle Coliseum fountain during my Army reserve time. Seattle was also a place where I worked briefly and experienced some of the political tensions during the Vietnam War era.
cues: bridge building over Seattle Coliseum fountain

summary: I attended Everett High School and graduated in 1961. While I didn’t play football, I did wrestle at 112 pounds. My high school years were spent in Everett, and it was a time when I balanced school, sports, and helping my uncle after school.

I served in the United States Army from 1962 to 1968, mostly as a reservist during the Vietnam War period. I trained at Fort Ord, California, and Fort Knox, Kentucky. My specialty was bridge building, and I was part of a tank brigade before moving to a bridge company in the reserves back home in Everett.

During the Vietnam War, I was a reservist in the Army and found the politics of the war confusing. My best friend served two tours in Vietnam as an officer. I experienced some of the anti-war protests firsthand while working at Scott Paper Company and serving in the reserves, which was a challenging time.

I did my basic training at Fort Ord, California for two months in 1962. It was the start of my military career before moving on to Fort Knox. Fort Ord was where I first experienced Army life and training as part of the tank brigade.

After Fort Ord, I spent four months at Fort Knox, Kentucky, continuing my Army training. It was there that I transitioned from tank brigade training to preparing for my reserve role in a bridge company back in Everett. Fort Knox was an important part of my military experience.

My best friend who served in Vietnam recently attended a reunion of officers and Vietnam veterans in Washington, D.C. This event was significant for those who served, and it connected us back to the experiences and bonds formed during the war. While I didn’t attend, it was important to me as part of my friend’s story.

After high school, I worked at Scott Paper Company for two years while attending Everett Junior College. During this time, I was also a weekend Army reservist. Working there gave me a sense of normalcy during the turbulent 1960s and was part of my early work experience before moving to Alaska.

I attended Everett Junior College for two years after high school while working at Scott Paper Company. This was between 1961 and 1964 before I moved to Alaska. It was a time of balancing work and education as I figured out my future path.

I moved to Anchorage, Alaska in 1964 to work in construction for my uncle's company. I worked in various places including Kenai, Big Lake, and Anchorage, building officers' quarters at Elmendorf Air Force Base and doing piledriving in Cook Inlet. It was a busy time in Alaska’s construction boom and my first experience with the Laborers' International Union of North America.

entity: Laborers' International Union of North America
summary: While working construction in Alaska, I paid dues to the Laborers' International Union of North America. However, it was very different from the ILWU; there were no meetings or union activities, just dues collection. I never attended a meeting or knew much about the union’s workings at that time.

During my time in Alaska, I worked in the Kenai Peninsula area as part of my construction jobs. This included various projects like piledriving and building infrastructure. It was part of the busy construction scene in Alaska in the mid-1960s.

Big Lake, Alaska was another location where I worked during my construction career in the 1960s. It was part of the spread of projects I was involved with before returning to Everett. The work was physically demanding and part of the Alaska construction boom.

Anchorage was my base when I moved to Alaska in 1964 to work in construction. I worked on projects around Anchorage, including Elmendorf Air Force Base. Anchorage was a hub of activity and the center of my life during those years before I returned to Everett in 1967.

At Elmendorf Air Force Base near Anchorage, I worked on building officers' quarters as part of my construction job in Alaska. This was one of the key projects during my time there from 1964 to 1967. It was a significant and challenging job site that gave me valuable experience.

I worked on piledriving projects in Cook Inlet, Alaska, including areas from Mount Alyeska to Homer. This was part of the heavy construction work I did in the mid-1960s before returning to Everett. The work was tough and involved infrastructure development in remote areas.

Mount Alyeska was part of the region where I worked on piledriving and construction projects in Alaska. It was along the route from there to Homer where we installed transform towers and other infrastructure. This was during my time in Alaska before 1967.

Homer was one of the endpoints of the construction work I did in Alaska, particularly in piledriving and installing transform towers. It was part of the Cook Inlet area projects I worked on before returning to Everett. The work was physically demanding and important for the region's development.

My first longshore job was on May 28, 1967, on the Kanoshima Maru, a log ship. It was a tough introduction to longshoring, working with logs that came in as they grew, and learning the ropes from experienced men. That first day was scary but it marked the start of my long and rewarding career on the waterfront.

While working on the waterfront, we handled a lot of pulp bales from Weyerhaeuser, which were heavy and demanding cargo. The work was dangerous and required skill to manage the 450-pound bales safely. Weyerhaeuser was a major part of the cargo we dealt with during my longshore career.

The Everett massacre was a significant event in the history of our union and the town where I grew up. Although I was not directly involved, it shaped the labor environment in Everett and the ILWU's fight for workers' rights. It remains a powerful memory in our union's history and community identity.

The 1971 ILWU strike was a major labor action that I experienced as a member of the union. It was a tough time for all of us on the waterfront, but it showed the strength and solidarity of the ILWU. The strike had a lasting impact on working conditions and union power along the coast.

entity: American Federation of Labor and Congress of Industrial Organizations
summary: My uncle belonged to a small union affiliated with the AFL-CIO, and when he died in 1977, we went to the AFL-CIO union hall in Everett for his death benefit. While I wasn’t deeply involved with the AFL-CIO myself, it was part of my family’s union background and the broader labor movement in Everett.
